figures.caption: Table 1 Summary of transfer learning strategies for fragility model adaptation.
Figure 1: Workflow and decision tree for selecting a transfer learning strategy in fragility model adaptation. The case studies illustrating each strategy are indicated as CS1, CS2, and CS3.
Table 2 Case Study I: predictors of the source model of Balomenos et al. (2020) used in Eq. (2).
Figure 2: Case Study I: source (𝑛𝑆 = 237) versus target (𝑛𝑇 = 29 raw inventory; 𝑛𝑇 = 27 after removing two surge-elevation outliers) marginal feature distributions.
Figure 3: Case Study I: Pearson correlation matrices of the predictor set in the source and target inventories.
Figure 4: Case Study I: instance-based TL source pool.

Figure 5: Case Study I: distribution of predicted failure probability 𝑃𝑓 , stratified by observed outcome and model. Boxplots summarize the predicted probability distributions for non-failure and failure observations for the three models reported in Table 3.
Figure 6: Case Study II: target-data imbalance and extended-feature selection diagnostics.
Figure 7: Case Study II: incremental F1 score versus target training sample size for five representative FFE categories (0, 3, 6, 7, 8).
Figure 8: Case Study II: confusion matrices for the four modeling scenarios (NF: not failed; F: failed).
Figure 9: Case Study II: Bayesian calibration curve with posterior draws and 95% credible band.
Table 6 Case Study III: base and adapted fragility parameters for Era 1 (pre-1970) bridges, 𝑆𝑎(𝑇 = 1.0 s).
Figure 10: Case Study III: confusion matrices for Era 1 (Pre-1970) bridges across the five models (damage-state classes 0–3).
Figure 11: Case Study III: marginal fragility curves for Era 1 (Pre-1970) bridges.

Figure 12: Case Study III: predicted failure probability 𝑃𝑓 = 𝑃 (𝐷𝑆 ≥ 𝑠) for Era 1 (Pre-1970) bridges, pooled over damage states and split by observed outcome (light box: not exceeded; dark box: exceeded).
